annotations.label: Prime ideal (informal)

anchor.snippet: a prime ideal is a subset of a ring that shares many important properties of a prime number

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Ideal.IsPrime I` as `I ≠ ⊤ ∧ ∀ {x y}, x * y ∈ I → x ∈ I ∨ y ∈ I`, exactly matching the standard prime-ideal concept this informal sentence describes.

annotations.label: Prime ideals of the integers

anchor.snippet: The prime ideals for the integers are the sets that contain all the multiples of a given prime number, together with the zero ideal

annotations.note: `Ideal.span_singleton_prime` gives `IsPrime (span {p}) ↔ Prime p` in any commutative ring; combined with `isPrime_bot` for `NoZeroDivisors`, this classifies the prime ideals of `ℤ` as `(p)` and `(0)`.

annotations.label: Primitive ideals are prime; prime ideals are primary and semiprime

anchor.snippet: Primitive ideals are prime, and prime ideals are both primary and semiprime

annotations.note: `IsPrime.isPrimary` and `IsPrime.isRadical` (in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Operations`) give the primary and semiprime/radical parts. Mathlib has no notion of `primitive ideal` (annihilator of a simple module) for noncommutative rings, so the first conjunct is not formalized.

annotations.label: Prime ideal of a commutative ring

anchor.snippet: An ideal P of a commutative ring R is prime if it has the following two properties

annotations.note: Mathlib's `class Ideal.IsPrime` has fields `ne_top'` (P ≠ R) and `mem_or_mem'` (x*y ∈ P → x ∈ P ∨ y ∈ P), and `isPrime_iff` gives the conjunction form. This matches Wikipedia's two-property definition verbatim (and works for any semiring).

annotations.label: Euclid's lemma for prime numbers

anchor.snippet: if p is a prime number and if p divides a product ab of two integers , then p divides a or p divides b

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime.dvd_mul` (and the alias `Prime.dvd_or_dvd` for the general `Prime` predicate) gives Euclid's lemma; an `Int` version is available via `Int.Prime.dvd_mul`.

annotations.label: n is a prime number iff (n) is a prime ideal in Z

anchor.snippet: A positive integer n is a prime number if and only if

annotations.note: `Ideal.span_singleton_prime : p ≠ 0 → (IsPrime (span {p}) ↔ Prime p)` proves the iff in any commutative ring; specializing to `R = ℤ` (with `Nat.prime_iff_prime_int`) gives the Wikipedia statement.

annotations.label: Prime spectrum of a commutative ring

anchor.snippet: The set of prime ideals of a commutative ring R is known as its (prime) spectrum

annotations.note: `PrimeSpectrum R` is defined as the structure bundling an ideal `asIdeal` with a proof `isPrime`; `equivSubtype` shows it is order-isomorphic to `{I : Ideal R // I.IsPrime}`.

annotations.label: Affine scheme (spectrum with topology and structure sheaf)

anchor.snippet: a topology and a sheaf of rings, that make it a geometric object known as an affine scheme

annotations.note: `AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme` is the category of affine schemes; the Zariski topology on `PrimeSpectrum R` is in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.Topology` and the structure sheaf is in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.StructureSheaf`.

annotations.label: Alternative definition of prime ideal (contrapositive form)
annotations.note: `IsPrime.mul_notMem : x ∉ I → y ∉ I → x*y ∉ I` is exactly the contrapositive form; the multiplicative-closure-of-complement viewpoint is also packaged as `Ideal.primeCompl : Submonoid α`.

anchor.snippet: A proper ideal I of R is prime if it has the following property

annotations.label: Equivalence of alternative definition via contrapositive

anchor.snippet: This property is mathematically equivalent to the standard definition used above as it was derived using the contrapositive

annotations.note: The equivalence is witnessed by `Ideal.primeCompl P : Submonoid α` (the complement of a prime ideal is closed under multiplication and contains 1), built directly from `IsPrime.mem_or_mem` and `one_notMem`.

annotations.label: Even integers form a prime ideal of Z

anchor.snippet: the subset of even numbers

annotations.note: `span_singleton_prime` with the prime element `2 : ℤ` shows `(2)` is a prime ideal of `ℤ` (and `Int.ideal_span_isMaximal_of_prime` makes it maximal). No standalone `2ℤ is prime` lemma; it is this special case.

annotations.label: Prime element of an integral domain generates a principal prime ideal

anchor.snippet: any prime element

annotations.note: `span_singleton_prime (hp : p ≠ 0) : IsPrime (span {p}) ↔ Prime p` says a prime element generates a principal prime ideal.

annotations.label: Irreducible polynomial over a field generates a prime ideal

anchor.snippet: take an irreducible polynomial

annotations.note: Over a field `K`, `K[X]` is a PID, so `isMaximal_of_irreducible` gives that an irreducible polynomial generates a maximal (hence prime, via `IsMaximal.isPrime`) ideal; in a PID/UFD irreducible = prime so `span_singleton_prime` also applies.

annotations.label: Eisenstein's criterion for irreducibility in polynomial rings over UFDs

anchor.snippet: Eisenstein's criterion for integral domains (hence UFDs ) can be effective for determining if an element in a polynomial ring is irreducible

annotations.note: `irreducible_of_eisenstein_criterion` is the classic Eisenstein criterion; `Polynomial.generalizedEisenstein` is a generalized form.

annotations.label: Ideal generated by Y^2 - X^3 - X - 1 in C[X,Y] is prime (elliptic curve)

anchor.snippet: the ideal generated by the polynomial Y 2 − X 3 − X − 1 is a prime ideal

annotations.note: This specific worked example (the elliptic-curve ideal in `ℂ[X,Y]` being prime) is not in Mathlib; the general machinery (`Ideal.IsPrime`, `span`) exists but not this concrete computation.

annotations.label: Ideal (2, X) in Z[X] is a prime ideal

anchor.snippet: of all polynomials with integer coefficients, the ideal

annotations.note: No standalone lemma that the specific ideal `(2, X) ⊆ ℤ[X]` is prime. Related tools exist (`Ideal.isPrime_map_C_iff_isPrime`; quotient ≅ 𝔽₂) but the concrete example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Maximal ideal

anchor.snippet: a maximal ideal is an ideal M that is maximal in the set of all proper ideals of R

annotations.note: `Ideal.IsMaximal I` is defined as `IsCoatom I`, i.e. maximal among proper ideals; `isMaximal_iff` unfolds it.

annotations.label: Every maximal ideal is prime

anchor.snippet: Every maximal ideal is in fact prime

annotations.note: `IsMaximal.isPrime` (and the instance `IsMaximal.isPrime'`) proves every maximal ideal of a commutative semiring is prime.

annotations.label: In a PID every nonzero prime ideal is maximal

anchor.snippet: In a principal ideal domain every nonzero prime ideal is maximal

annotations.note: A PID is a domain of Krull dimension ≤ 1, so `IsPrime.isMaximal_of_ne_bot` ([NoZeroDivisors][KrullDimLE 1]) gives nonzero prime ⇒ maximal; also `Ideal.IsPrime.isMaximal` for `DimensionLEOne` domains. No lemma stated directly under `IsPrincipalIdealRing`.

annotations.label: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz: form of maximal ideals in k[x_1,...,x_n]

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz states that every maximal ideal is of the form

annotations.note: Over an algebraically closed field, `isMaximal_iff_eq_vanishingIdeal_singleton` says maximal ideals of `MvPolynomial σ K` are exactly vanishing ideals of points; the main statement is `vanishingIdeal_zeroLocus_eq_radical`.

annotations.label: Vanishing functions at a point on a smooth manifold form a (maximal) prime ideal

anchor.snippet: the set of all smooth functions f with f ( x ) = 0 forms a prime ideal

annotations.note: Mathlib has no development of the ring of smooth functions on a manifold as an algebraic object, so the ideal of functions vanishing at a point (and its primality/maximality) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-example: quotient ring is not an integral domain

anchor.snippet: the last is not an integral domain since it is isomorphic to

annotations.note: `Ideal.Quotient.isDomain_iff_prime : IsDomain (R ⧸ I) ↔ I.IsPrime` is the general principle whose negation Wikipedia is using on the specific non-example.

annotations.label: Non-example: factorization yields zero divisors in the quotient (via CRT)

anchor.snippet: which implies the existence of zero divisors in the quotient ring, preventing it from being isomorphic to

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the general iff `IsDomain (R/I) ↔ I.IsPrime` and has CRT (`Ideal.quotientInfRingEquivPiQuotient`), but the specific worked computation showing the quotient has zero divisors is not packaged as a lemma.

annotations.label: Non-example: the ideal is not prime

anchor.snippet: This shows that the ideal

annotations.note: The specific concrete non-example (a particular non-prime ideal verified in the Wikipedia text) is not stated as a lemma in Mathlib; only the general criterion `Ideal.Quotient.isDomain_iff_prime` is available.

annotations.label: Non-example: product lies in ideal but neither factor does

anchor.snippet: but neither

annotations.note: The specific worked counterexample exhibiting an `a*b ∈ I` with `a, b ∉ I` is not a Mathlib lemma; the abstract framework `not_isPrime_iff` (in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Prime`) gives the schema but no concrete instantiation is provided.

annotations.label: I is prime iff R/I is an integral domain

anchor.snippet: An ideal I in the ring R (with unity ) is prime if and only if the factor ring R / I is an integral domain

annotations.note: `Ideal.Quotient.isDomain_iff_prime : IsDomain (R ⧸ I) ↔ I.IsPrime`.

annotations.label: Commutative ring is an integral domain iff (0) is prime

anchor.snippet: a commutative ring (with unity) is an integral domain if and only if (0) is a prime ideal

annotations.note: Both directions: `IsDomain.of_bot_isPrime` (⊥ prime ⇒ domain) and the instance `isPrime_bot` ([Nontrivial][NoZeroDivisors] ⇒ ⊥ prime).

annotations.label: Zero ring has no prime ideals

anchor.snippet: The zero ring has no prime ideals, because the ideal (0) is the whole ring

annotations.note: The prime spectrum of the zero ring is empty (`IsEmpty (PrimeSpectrum R)` for a `Subsingleton` ring): any prime needs `I ≠ ⊤`, but `⊤ = ⊥` there.

annotations.label: I is prime iff its complement is multiplicatively closed

anchor.snippet: An ideal I is prime if and only if its set-theoretic complement is multiplicatively closed

annotations.note: `Ideal.primeCompl` packages the complement of a prime ideal as a `Submonoid` (multiplicatively closed, contains 1) — the forward direction. The full iff (closed + proper ⇒ prime) is not a single named lemma.

annotations.label: Every nonzero ring contains a prime (and maximal) ideal (Krull's theorem)

anchor.snippet: Every nonzero ring contains at least one prime ideal

annotations.note: `exists_maximal [Nontrivial α]` gives Krull's theorem (a nontrivial ring has a maximal ideal); with `IsMaximal.isPrime` this yields a prime ideal.

annotations.label: Krull's lemma: ideal maximal disjoint from a multiplicatively closed set is prime

anchor.snippet: if S is any multiplicatively closed set in R , then a lemma essentially due to Krull shows that there exists an ideal of R maximal with respect to being disjoint from S , and moreover the ideal must be prime

annotations.note: `isPrime_of_maximally_disjoint`: an ideal maximally disjoint from a `Submonoid S` is prime (the primality conclusion of Krull's lemma).

annotations.label: Positive powers of a non-nilpotent element form a prototypical m-system

anchor.snippet: Another prototypical m-system is the set

annotations.note: Powers of an element are `Submonoid.powers` (a multiplicatively closed set); disjointness from a prime relates to non-nilpotency (`disjoint_powers_iff_notMem`). The m-system framing is not formalized.

annotations.label: Preimage of a prime ideal under a ring homomorphism is prime

anchor.snippet: The preimage of a prime ideal under a ring homomorphism is a prime ideal

annotations.note: Instance `IsPrime.comap` (and explicit `comap_isPrime`): `comap f K` is prime when `K` is prime.

annotations.label: Preimage of a maximal ideal need not be maximal

anchor.snippet: The analogous fact is not always true for maximal ideals

annotations.note: Mathlib has positive results (e.g. `IsMaximal.comap_bijective`) but no formalized counterexample that `comap` of a maximal ideal can fail to be maximal.

annotations.label: Minimal prime ideal

anchor.snippet: The set of all prime ideals (called the spectrum of a ring ) contains minimal elements (called minimal prime ideals )

annotations.note: `Ideal.IsMinimalPrime I p` (minimal prime over `I`) and the abbreviation `IsMinimalPrime p = (⊥).IsMinimalPrime p`; existence via `exists_minimalPrimes_le`.

annotations.label: Minimal primes correspond to irreducible components of the spectrum

anchor.snippet: Geometrically, these correspond to irreducible components of the spectrum

annotations.note: `minimalPrimes.equivIrreducibleComponents` gives the correspondence between minimal primes and irreducible components of `Spec`.

annotations.label: Sum of two prime ideals is not necessarily prime

anchor.snippet: The sum of two prime ideals is not necessarily prime

annotations.note: No formalized counterexample that the sum (`⊔`) of two prime ideals can fail to be prime.

annotations.label: Not every irreducible ideal is prime

anchor.snippet: Not every ideal which cannot be factored into two ideals is a prime ideal

annotations.note: Mathlib has no predicate for `irreducible ideal` in this factorization sense, nor a counterexample distinguishing it from prime.

annotations.label: Commutative ring in which every proper ideal is prime is a field

anchor.snippet: if every proper ideal is prime, then the ring is a field

annotations.note: Related characterizations exist (`isField_iff_maximal_bot`, `not_isField_iff_exists_prime`), but the exact statement `every proper ideal prime ⇒ field` is not a stated lemma.

annotations.label: Nonzero principal ideal is prime iff generated by a prime element

anchor.snippet: A nonzero principal ideal is prime if and only if it is generated by a prime element

annotations.note: `span_singleton_prime (hp : p ≠ 0) : IsPrime (span {p}) ↔ Prime p`.

annotations.label: In a UFD, every nonzero prime ideal contains a prime element

anchor.snippet: In a UFD, every nonzero prime ideal contains a prime element

annotations.note: Kaplansky's criterion (`iff_exists_prime_mem_of_isPrime`): a domain is a UFD iff every nonzero prime ideal contains a prime element; the forward direction gives exactly this property.

annotations.label: Irreducible varieties correspond to prime ideals

anchor.snippet: the irreducible varieties correspond to prime ideals

annotations.note: `isIrreducible_iff_vanishingIdeal_isPrime` states that a closed subset of `PrimeSpectrum R` is irreducible iff its vanishing ideal is prime — the scheme-theoretic incarnation of the Wikipedia correspondence.

annotations.label: Scheme (generalization of variety via spectrum of prime ideals)

anchor.snippet: turns the set of its prime ideals, also called its spectrum , into a topological space and can thus define generalizations of varieties called schemes

annotations.note: `structure Scheme extends LocallyRingedSpace` defines a scheme as a locally ringed space locally isomorphic to `Spec R`, exactly the generalization Wikipedia describes.

annotations.label: Unique factorization fails in some rings of algebraic integers

anchor.snippet: unique factorisation expressed in the fundamental theorem of arithmetic does not hold in every ring of algebraic integers

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes `UniqueFactorizationMonoid` and Dedekind domains, and proves rings of integers are Dedekind, but the specific negative existential `∃ K, ¬UniqueFactorizationMonoid (𝓞 K)` (e.g. for `ℚ(√−5)`) is not present as a lemma.

annotations.label: Prime ideal in a noncommutative ring

anchor.snippet: we say that P is prime if for any two ideals A and B of R

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Ideal.IsPrime` uses the elementwise commutative form (`x*y ∈ P → x ∈ P ∨ y ∈ P`), and there is no two-sided-ideal `AB ⊆ P → A ⊆ P ∨ B ⊆ P` definition for noncommutative rings (the file's own TODO notes this gap).

annotations.label: Noncommutative definition is equivalent to the commutative one in commutative rings

anchor.snippet: this definition is equivalent to the commutative one in commutative rings

annotations.note: Without a noncommutative `IsPrime` predicate in Mathlib, the equivalence to the commutative definition has no statement to prove.

annotations.label: Commutative-prime ideal in a noncommutative ring is also prime in the noncommutative sense

anchor.snippet: if an ideal of a noncommutative ring R satisfies the commutative definition of prime, then it also satisfies the noncommutative version

annotations.note: No noncommutative `IsPrime` predicate exists in Mathlib, so this implication is not formalized.

annotations.label: Completely prime ideal

anchor.snippet: sometimes called a completely prime ideal to distinguish it from other merely prime ideals

annotations.note: The notion of `completely prime ideal` (specific to the noncommutative theory) has no separate definition in Mathlib; only the commutative-style `Ideal.IsPrime` exists.

annotations.label: Completely prime ideals are prime, but the converse is false

anchor.snippet: Completely prime ideals are prime ideals, but the converse is not true

annotations.note: Mathlib has neither `completely prime` nor a noncommutative `IsPrime` predicate, so this implication and its counterexample are absent.

annotations.label: Zero ideal of n x n matrix ring over a field is prime but not completely prime

anchor.snippet: the zero ideal in the ring of n

annotations.note: Although Mathlib defines matrix rings and knows `Matrix (Fin n) (Fin n) k` is simple over a field, the specific statement that `(⊥)` is prime-but-not-completely-prime in matrix rings is not present (and `completely prime` is undefined).

annotations.label: Equivalent formulations of P being prime (noncommutative ring)

anchor.snippet: Equivalent formulations of the ideal

annotations.note: The list of equivalent formulations of `P` prime in the noncommutative sense is not formalized because the underlying noncommutative `IsPrime` predicate is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalent formulation: principal-ideal-product condition

anchor.snippet: For all a and b in R

annotations.note: The condition `(a)(b) ⊆ P → a ∈ P ∨ b ∈ P` in the noncommutative sense is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalent formulation: right-ideal product condition

anchor.snippet: For any two right ideals of R

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize one-sided right ideals as a first-class concept, and the right-ideal-product characterization of noncommutative-prime is absent.

annotations.label: Equivalent formulation: left-ideal product condition

anchor.snippet: For any two left ideals of R

annotations.note: Analogous to the right-ideal case: Mathlib does not have a left-ideal-product characterization of (noncommutative) prime ideals.

annotations.label: Equivalent formulation: aRb subset of P condition

anchor.snippet: For any elements a and b of R , if aRb

annotations.note: The `aRb ⊆ P → a ∈ P ∨ b ∈ P` characterization (the key noncommutative reformulation) is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: m-system

anchor.snippet: is called an m-system if for any a and b in S , there exists r in R such that arb is in S

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `m-system` definition. The closely related commutative notion — `primeCompl` being a `Submonoid` — appears in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Prime`, but a true m-system (closed under `arb` for some `r`) is absent.

annotations.label: Complement of a prime ideal is an m-system

anchor.snippet: The complement R

annotations.note: For (semi)commutative rings, `Ideal.primeCompl` shows the complement of a prime ideal is a multiplicative submonoid — the commutative special case of the m-system statement. The noncommutative m-system version itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Any primitive ideal is prime

anchor.snippet: Any primitive ideal is prime

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of primitive ideal (annihilator of a simple module), so this is not formalized.

annotations.label: Maximal ideals are prime; prime ideals contain minimal primes (noncommutative)

anchor.snippet: maximal ideals are prime, and also prime ideals contain minimal prime ideals

annotations.note: In the commutative setting both hold: `IsMaximal.isPrime`, and `exists_minimalPrimes_le` shows every prime over `I` contains a minimal prime over `I`. The noncommutative two-sided version is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ring is prime iff (0) is prime; ring is a domain iff (0) is completely prime

anchor.snippet: A ring is a prime ring if and only if the zero ideal is a prime ideal

annotations.note: The `domain iff ⊥ completely prime` half is `IsDomain.of_bot_isPrime` / `isPrime_bot`. There is no `prime ring` predicate in Mathlib, so the noncommutative `prime ring iff ⊥ prime` half is not formalized.

annotations.label: Maximal annihilator of a submodule of a nonzero module is prime

anchor.snippet: if A is a nonzero R - module , and P is a maximal element in the poset of annihilator ideals of submodules of A , then P is prime

annotations.note: The associated-prime machinery (`IsAssociatedPrime`, `exists_le_isAssociatedPrime_of_isNoetherianRing`) shows a maximal annihilator (colon) ideal is prime — the Noetherian version of this fact.

annotations.label: Prime avoidance lemma

anchor.snippet: Prime avoidance lemma

annotations.note: `subset_union_prime` is prime avoidance (Atiyah-Macdonald 1.11): if `I ⊆ ⋃ f i` with all but two `f i` prime, then `I ≤ f i` for some `i`.

annotations.label: Prime not containing any I_j does not contain their intersection

anchor.snippet: if P does not contain any I j , it also does not contain the intersection

annotations.note: `IsPrime.inf_le' : (⨅ i ∈ s, f i) ≤ P ↔ ∃ i ∈ s, f i ≤ P` (and binary `IsPrime.inf_le`); contrapositive gives that a prime not containing any `I_j` does not contain their intersection.

annotations.label: Krull's lemma (m-system version): ideal maximal disjoint from m-system is prime

anchor.snippet: If S is any m-system in R , then a lemma essentially due to Krull shows that there exists an ideal I of R maximal with respect to being disjoint from S , and moreover the ideal I must be prime

annotations.note: Commutative version (maximal disjoint from a `Submonoid` ⇒ prime) is `isPrime_of_maximally_disjoint`. The m-system (noncommutative) generalization is not formalized since m-systems are absent.

annotations.label: Positive powers of a non-nilpotent element form a prototypical m-system

anchor.snippet: Another prototypical m-system is the set

annotations.note: Powers of an element form `Submonoid.powers`; disjointness from a prime relates to non-nilpotency (`disjoint_powers_iff_notMem`). The m-system framing itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Saturated subset (closed under divisors)

anchor.snippet: A set that contains the divisors of its elements is called saturated

annotations.note: Mathlib has localization-specific saturation notions but no general `saturated subset (closed under divisors)` definition matching this article's usage.

annotations.label: Complement of a prime ideal is saturated and multiplicatively closed

anchor.snippet: the complement R ∖ P has another property beyond being an m-system

annotations.note: `primeCompl` shows the complement is a submonoid (multiplicatively closed). The `saturated` (closed under divisors) property is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Complement of a saturated multiplicatively closed set is a union of prime ideals

anchor.snippet: If S is any nonempty saturated and multiplicatively closed subset of R , the complement R ∖ S is the union of prime ideals of R

annotations.note: This structure theorem (complement of a saturated mult. closed set is a union of primes) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intersection (and in commutative case, union) of a chain of prime ideals is prime

anchor.snippet: The intersection of members of a chain of prime ideals is a prime ideal

annotations.note: `sInf_isPrime_of_isChain` proves the intersection of a nonempty chain of primes is prime. The union (`sSup`) of a chain of primes being prime is not formalized as a named lemma.

annotations.label: Poset of prime ideals has maximal and minimal elements (via Zorn)

anchor.snippet: the poset of prime ideals of a commutative ring (partially ordered by inclusion) has maximal and minimal elements

annotations.note: Existence of minimal primes below any prime is `exists_minimalPrimes_le`; maximal ideals (maximal primes) exist by `exists_maximal`. No single lemma packages `the prime poset has both maximal and minimal elements`.

annotations.label: Prime ideals arise as maximal elements of certain collections of ideals

anchor.snippet: Prime ideals can frequently be produced as maximal elements of certain collections of ideals

annotations.note: `IsOka.isPrime_of_maximal_not` formalizes the general principle: an ideal maximal w.r.t. failing any Oka predicate is prime. `isPrime_of_maximally_disjoint` (in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Maximal`) is another instance of producing primes as maximal elements.

annotations.label: Ideal maximal w.r.t. empty intersection with a fixed m-system is prime

anchor.snippet: An ideal maximal with respect to having empty intersection with a fixed m-system is prime

annotations.note: `isPrime_of_maximally_disjoint` proves: in a commutative (semi)ring, an ideal maximal among those disjoint from a `Submonoid S` is prime. This is the commutative special case (submonoid in place of general m-system) of the Wikipedia statement.

annotations.label: Ideal maximal among annihilators of submodules of a fixed R-module is prime

anchor.snippet: An ideal maximal among annihilators of submodules of a fixed R -module M is prime

annotations.note: `exists_le_isAssociatedPrime_of_isNoetherianRing` constructs an associated prime as an ideal maximal among annihilator colon ideals `(⊥ : Submodule R M).colon {y}` and shows it is prime — formalizing the Wikipedia statement in the Noetherian setting.

annotations.label: In a commutative ring, an ideal maximal w.r.t. being non-principal is prime

anchor.snippet: an ideal maximal with respect to being non-principal is prime

annotations.note: `isOka_isPrincipal : IsOka Submodule.IsPrincipal` combined with `IsOka.isPrime_of_maximal_not` gives exactly: an ideal maximal among non-principal ideals is prime (the engine of `IsPrincipalIdealRing.of_prime`).

annotations.label: In a commutative ring, an ideal maximal w.r.t. being not countably generated is prime

anchor.snippet: an ideal maximal with respect to being not countably generated is prime

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the finitely-generated analog: `isOka_fg` plus `IsOka.isPrime_of_maximal_not` says a maximal-not-fg ideal is prime (used in `IsNoetherianRing.of_prime`). The `countably generated` variant from Wikipedia is not directly present.
